title: Learning & Development
simple_html:
Learning and development (L&D) is how organizations build the skills, knowledge and behaviours their people need — through training, coaching, on-the-job experience and education. It is the main way to grow the Ability that drives performance.

Why it matters

Markets, technology and roles change fast. L&D keeps the workforce capable, helps people grow and stay, and turns hiring potential into real performance. It is an investment in human capital — the value carried in people's skills.

The training cycle

Good L&D follows a simple loop, often called ADDIE or the training cycle:

  1. Identify needs — what skills gap exists, at the organization, job and person levels?
  2. Design — set learning objectives and choose methods.
  3. Deliver — run the training (workshop, e-learning, on-the-job, coaching).
  4. Evaluate — did it work? Did behaviour and results improve?

How people learn

Much learning happens by doing, not in classrooms. A popular rule of thumb is 70:20:10 — about 70% from on-the-job experience, 20% from others (coaching, mentoring), and 10% from formal courses.

Key takeaways

  • L&D builds the Ability behind performance.
  • Follow the cycle: needs → design → deliver → evaluate.
  • Most learning is experiential (70:20:10), not classroom-only.
  • Always measure results, not just attendance or smile-sheets.
  • Linking L&D to careers helps retain good people.
intermediate_html:
Learning and development (L&D) is the organizational function that builds workforce capability — knowledge, skills, attitudes and behaviours — to meet current and future needs. This lesson covers the systematic training cycle, needs analysis, adult-learning principles, delivery methods including 70:20:10, and rigorous evaluation, with a worked example tying it to performance.

1. From training to learning to development

The terms differ in scope. Training is structured activity to build specific job skills (short-term, job-focused). Development is broader, longer-term growth of the person and their potential. Learning is the underlying process of acquiring capability, much of it informal. The modern shift is from "training events" toward continuous learning embedded in work and a "learning organization" culture (Senge, 1990).

2. The systematic training cycle / ADDIE

Effective L&D is systematic. The ADDIE model — Analyse, Design, Develop, Implement, Evaluate — and the classic four-stage training cycle (identify needs → design → deliver → evaluate) ensure training solves real problems rather than being activity for its own sake.

3. Training needs analysis (TNA)

TNA diagnoses where learning will add value, conventionally at three levels (McGehee & Thayer, 1961): organizational (which capabilities does strategy require?), task/operational (what does effective performance of the job involve?), and person (who needs what, comparing current vs required performance?). The output is specific, measurable learning objectives. A frequent error is assuming training is the answer when the real cause is motivation, tools or process — TNA prevents wasted spend.

4. Adult learning principles

Design should reflect how adults learn. Andragogy (Knowles, 1984) notes adults are self-directed, bring experience, and learn best when content is relevant and problem-centred. Kolb's experiential learning cycle (1984) — concrete experience, reflective observation, abstract conceptualization, active experimentation — implies learning needs both doing and reflection. These principles favour active, applied, spaced learning over passive lecture.

5. Delivery methods and 70:20:10

Methods span on-the-job (coaching, mentoring, job rotation, stretch assignments, action learning) and off-the-job (workshops, e-learning, simulations, MOOCs, qualifications). The 70:20:10 framework (Lombardo & Eichinger) is a rule of thumb that roughly 70% of development comes from challenging experience, 20% from others, and 10% from formal courses — a reminder to design experiential and social learning, not just courses. Transfer of learning — applying it back on the job — is the critical, often-neglected step, supported by manager involvement, relevance and opportunity to practise.

6. Evaluation: the Kirkpatrick model

Evaluation closes the loop. Kirkpatrick's four levels are: (1) Reaction (did learners like it?), (2) Learning (did they acquire it?), (3) Behaviour (do they apply it on the job?), and (4) Results (did it affect organizational outcomes?). Phillips adds (5) ROI. Most organizations stop at Level 1 "smile-sheets"; real value requires measuring behaviour and results.

7. Worked example

A retailer with falling customer-satisfaction scores runs a TNA: the organizational need is service quality; the task analysis shows complaint-handling gaps; the person level shows newer staff lack scripts and confidence. Objectives are set ("resolve 90% of complaints at first contact"). Delivery blends a short workshop (10%), peer coaching on the floor (20%) and live supervised practice (70%). Evaluation tracks Level 2 (role-play scores), Level 3 (mystery-shopper behaviour) and Level 4 (CSAT and repeat-purchase) — CSAT rises 8 points, demonstrating transfer and impact rather than mere attendance.

8. How to apply it

  • Start with TNA at organizational, task and person levels; confirm training is the right fix.
  • Write measurable objectives and design with adult-learning principles.
  • Use 70:20:10 — engineer experiential and social learning, not just courses.
  • Plan for transfer (manager support, practice, relevance).
  • Evaluate to Kirkpatrick Levels 3-4, not just reactions.

L&D builds the Ability lever of AMO (Lesson 2), follows on from Recruitment & Selection and feeds Performance Management and talent/career planning.
full_html:
Learning and development (L&D) — also termed human resource development (HRD) — is the systematic, strategic process of building the knowledge, skills, abilities and behaviours of an organization's people to enhance current performance and future capability (Armstrong & Taylor, 2020). As the primary mechanism for growing human capital and the AMO Ability lever, L&D connects directly to competitive advantage. This lesson develops the theory of learning, the systematic cycle and needs analysis, contemporary delivery models, the transfer problem, rigorous evaluation with seminal frameworks, the strategic learning-organization view, and common pitfalls.

1. Concepts and strategic rationale

Training, development and education differ in scope and time horizon, but the unifying construct is learning — a relatively permanent change in capability arising from experience. The strategic case rests on human capital theory (Becker, 1964): investment in people's skills raises productivity, with the key distinction between general human capital (transferable, so individuals tend to fund it) and firm-specific human capital (non-transferable, so firms are willing to fund it and it aids retention). In a knowledge economy with rapid skills obsolescence, continuous reskilling and upskilling become a survival capability, and L&D shifts from discrete events toward embedded, continuous, self-directed learning.

2. Theories of learning

Several traditions inform design. Behaviourism (reinforcement, practice, feedback) underpins drills and competency-based training. Cognitivism emphasizes how information is processed, structured and retained (chunking, spacing, retrieval practice). Constructivism holds that learners actively build meaning from experience. Andragogy (Knowles, 1984) specifies adult-learning assumptions: self-direction, the role of prior experience, readiness tied to life/role relevance, problem-centred orientation and internal motivation. Kolb's experiential learning cycle (1984) — concrete experience → reflective observation → abstract conceptualization → active experimentation — stresses that effective learning integrates doing with reflection and theorizing, not one alone. Social learning theory (Bandura, 1977) explains learning through observation, modelling and self-efficacy, justifying coaching, mentoring and communities of practice.

3. The systematic cycle and needs analysis

L&D should follow a systematic process — the four-stage training cycle or the ADDIE model (Analyse, Design, Develop, Implement, Evaluate). The foundation is training needs analysis. McGehee & Thayer's (1961) three-level framework remains standard: organizational analysis (strategy, culture, resources — which capabilities are required and is the climate supportive of transfer?), task/operational analysis (the KSAs effective performance demands), and person analysis (who has gaps, comparing actual vs desired performance). A central diagnostic discipline is distinguishing genuine skill/knowledge gaps (which training can fix) from problems of motivation, incentives, tools or process (which it cannot) — a classic Mager & Pipe (1970) point: "could they do it if their life depended on it?" If yes, the issue is not training.

4. Delivery methods and the 70:20:10 model

Methods range across a continuum: on-the-job (coaching, mentoring, job rotation, stretch and stretch assignments, action learning [Revans], shadowing) and off-the-job (instructor-led workshops, e-learning, blended learning, simulations, MOOCs, microlearning, formal qualifications). The widely cited 70:20:10 framework (Lombardo & Eichinger, drawing on Center for Creative Leadership research) proposes that development comes roughly 70% from challenging on-the-job experiences, 20% from developmental relationships, and 10% from formal coursework. It is best treated as a heuristic, not a precise law — its empirical base is contested — but its design implication is sound and important: privilege experiential and social learning and embed learning in the flow of work rather than relying on classroom events.

5. Transfer of training — the critical bottleneck

The chronic failure point is transfer of learning: capability acquired in training is often not applied on the job. Baldwin & Ford's (1988) model identifies three determinants of transfer: trainee characteristics (ability, motivation, self-efficacy), training design (relevance, identical elements, practice, behavioural modelling), and the work environment (supervisor support, peer support, opportunity to perform, transfer climate). Practically, manager involvement before and after training and the opportunity to apply learning (the AMO Opportunity lever again) are decisive — training without a supportive transfer climate is wasted spend.

6. Evaluation: Kirkpatrick, Phillips and beyond

Evaluation closes the cycle. Kirkpatrick's (1959) four levels — Reaction (satisfaction), Learning (knowledge/skill acquired), Behaviour (on-the-job application), and Results (organizational impact) — remain the dominant model; Phillips (1997) adds a fifth, ROI (monetary benefits vs costs). Critiques note the levels are not necessarily causally linked (liking a course does not imply learning or impact) and that practice over-relies on Level 1 "happy sheets." The Brinkerhoff Success Case Method and modern learning analytics push toward credibly measuring behaviour and results. Rigorous evaluation requires baselines, control/comparison where feasible, and isolating training's contribution from other factors.

7. The learning organization and strategic HRD

At the systemic level, Senge's (1990) learning organization (personal mastery, mental models, shared vision, team learning, systems thinking) and Argyris & Schön's (1978) single- vs double-loop learning (correcting actions vs questioning underlying assumptions) frame L&D as an organizational, not merely individual, capability. Strategic HRD aligns the learning architecture to business strategy and to talent and succession planning, treating capability building as a source of dynamic advantage.

8. Worked example with evaluation

A manufacturer adopting lean methods runs a TNA: organizational need = defect reduction and continuous-improvement culture; task analysis = problem-solving and root-cause skills; person analysis = supervisors lack facilitation skills. Objectives: "supervisors run weekly kaizen events and reduce line defects 20% in six months." Design uses 70:20:10 — a two-day workshop (10%), paired coaching by a lean champion (20%), and live kaizen facilitation with feedback (70%) — with managers committing in advance to protect kaizen time (transfer climate). Evaluation: Level 2 (skills assessment), Level 3 (observed facilitation quality and event frequency), Level 4 (defect rate falls 24%), and Level 5 (ROI: savings vs program cost ≈ 3:1). The case shows that impact came from transfer support and experiential design, not the workshop alone.

9. Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Training as a default solution — applying training to non-training problems (motivation, process, tools) wastes resources.
  • Skipping TNA — "sheep-dip" training everyone regardless of need.
  • Ignoring transfer — no manager support or opportunity to apply, so learning decays.
  • Evaluating only reactions — smile-sheets prove popularity, not impact.
  • Treating 70:20:10 as exact — it is a design heuristic, not a measured constant.
  • Confusing activity with learning — hours logged or courses completed are inputs, not outcomes.

10. Cross-links

L&D is the core mechanism for the Ability lever of AMO (Lesson 2) and complements HR Planning, Recruitment & Selection (Lesson 3) by closing capability gaps recruitment cannot. It supplies development plans arising from Performance Management (Lesson 4), underpins career and succession work in Talent, Wellbeing & HR Analytics (Lesson 8), and supports engagement and retention discussed in Employee Relations & Engagement (Lesson 7). Mastery means running a systematic, needs-driven cycle that designs for adult learning and transfer and evaluates credibly through to behaviour and results.
